seoTitle: TDK-Lambda Technical Support | Power Supply Selection & Application Guides
seoDescription: Access TDK-Lambda technical documentation, power supply selection guides, and application notes. Expert FAE support for power system design and integration from authorized distributor.

faqs.question: Where can I find TDK-Lambda product documentation and datasheets?
faqs.answer: TDK-Lambda product documentation is available through multiple channels: LiTong Electronics website - comprehensive product pages with datasheets, application notes, and technical guides; TDK-Lambda website - complete technical documentation library; Direct contact - our FAE team can provide specific documents and answer technical questions. All documentation includes detailed specifications, electrical characteristics, mechanical drawings, and application guidance. Contact us for the latest documentation or specific technical resources.
faqs.decisionGuide: Contact our technical support team for documentation requests or specific technical resources.

faqs.question: How do I get technical support for my TDK-Lambda power supply design?
faqs.answer: LiTong Electronics provides comprehensive technical support for TDK-Lambda power supply designs: Application engineering - power supply selection, thermal analysis, and system integration; Product selection - helping choose optimal power supply series for your requirements; Design validation - schematic review, PCB layout guidance for power components; Production support - manufacturing guidance and quality assurance. Support channels: Direct FAE contact for complex designs; Email support for documentation and general questions; Phone support for urgent issues. Our FAE team has extensive experience with TDK-Lambda products and can guide you through the entire design process.
faqs.decisionGuide: Contact our FAE team to discuss your design requirements and support needs.

faqs.question: What design tools are available for TDK-Lambda power supplies?
faqs.answer: TDK-Lambda provides several design tools to assist with power supply selection and application: Online product selectors - filter by specifications to find the right power supply; Power calculators - for estimating efficiency, thermal performance, and reliability; Application notes - detailed design guidance for specific applications; MTBF calculators - for reliability predictions. Access: Most tools available through LiTong FAE team; Online calculators available on request; FAE team can assist with tool usage and interpretation of results. Contact us for tool access and training.
faqs.decisionGuide: Contact our FAE team for design tool access and assistance with power supply calculations.

faqs.question: How do I select the right TDK-Lambda power supply for my application?
faqs.answer: Selecting the right TDK-Lambda power supply involves several key steps: First, determine your input voltage range and type (AC or DC); Second, calculate your output power requirements with appropriate margin (typically 20-30%); Third, identify required safety certifications (industrial IEC 62368-1 or medical IEC 60601-1); Fourth, consider environmental conditions including operating temperature range; Fifth, evaluate mechanical requirements such as form factor and mounting; Sixth, assess special features needed such as remote sensing, parallel operation, or digital communication. TDK-Lambda offers selection tools, and our FAE team can provide personalized recommendations.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use our power supply selection guide or contact our FAE team for personalized product recommendations.

faqs.question: What is the process for getting TDK-Lambda power supply samples?
faqs.answer: LiTong Electronics provides TDK-Lambda power supply samples for evaluation: Sample request process: Contact our sales or FAE team with your requirements; Provide project information and expected production volumes; Samples typically ship within 1-3 business days for stock items; Evaluation boards available for some product families. Evaluation support: We provide evaluation kits with documentation; Technical support for evaluation and testing; FAE support for application-specific questions. For production planning, we recommend requesting samples early in the design cycle.
faqs.decisionGuide: Contact our sales team to request samples and discuss your evaluation requirements.

faqs.question: How do I calculate power supply efficiency and thermal design?
faqs.answer: Power supply efficiency and thermal design calculations: Efficiency calculation - η = Pout / Pin, or Ploss = Pout × (1-η)/η; Heat generation - calculate power dissipation from efficiency; Temperature rise - ΔT = Ploss × Rthermal where Rthermal is thermal resistance; Maximum ambient - Tambient_max = Trated_max - ΔT. Example: 1000W supply at 94% efficiency generates 64W heat; With thermal resistance of 2°C/W, temperature rise is 128°C. Thermal management options: Natural convection, forced air cooling, heatsinks, or baseplate mounting. Our FAE team can perform detailed thermal analysis for your specific application.
faqs.decisionGuide: Contact our FAE team for thermal calculations and optimal cooling recommendations.

faqs.question: What training resources are available for TDK-Lambda power supplies?
faqs.answer: LiTong Electronics offers various training resources for TDK-Lambda power supplies: Technical seminars - periodic training sessions on power supply technology and applications; Application notes - detailed guides for specific applications and design techniques; Online resources - webinars, video tutorials, and technical articles; FAE consultations - one-on-one training for your specific application. Topics covered include: Power supply selection and sizing; Thermal design and reliability; EMI and EMC considerations; Medical power supply design; Industrial power applications. Contact us to schedule training.
faqs.decisionGuide: Contact our FAE team to schedule training or request specific technical education resources.

faqs.question: How do I troubleshoot power supply issues in my design?
faqs.answer: Troubleshooting power supply issues involves systematic diagnosis: Check input voltage - ensure within specified range; Measure output voltage - verify regulation and ripple; Check temperature - verify operating temperature is within rating; Inspect physical condition - look for overheating, damage, or loose connections; Measure efficiency - compare to datasheet specifications. Common issues: Overheating - caused by insufficient cooling or overload; Output ripple - may indicate capacitor aging or insufficient filtering; Regulation problems - check load conditions and remote sense connections. Our FAE team can assist with troubleshooting complex issues.
faqs.decisionGuide: Contact our FAE team for troubleshooting assistance and root cause analysis.

articles.title: How to Select TDK-Lambda AC-DC Power Supplies
articles.summary: Comprehensive guide for selecting TDK-Lambda AC-DC power supplies based on power requirements, safety certifications, and environmental conditions.
articles.content: This guide provides a systematic approach to selecting TDK-Lambda AC-DC power supplies for your specific application requirements. AC-DC power supplies are the foundation of most electronic systems, converting AC mains power to regulated DC for electronic circuits. Step 1: Determine Power Requirements. Calculate total system power consumption including all loads and add 20-30% margin for reliability and future expansion. Step 2: Select Input Voltage Range. Choose between narrow range (e.g., 90-132VAC for North America) or universal input (85-264VAC) for global deployment. Step 3: Choose Safety Certifications. Select industrial (IEC 62368-1) or medical (IEC 60601-1) certifications based on application. Step 4: Consider Environmental Requirements. Operating temperature range, cooling method, and enclosure requirements affect selection.

faeInsights.insight: The most common mistake in power supply selection is undersizing for thermal conditions. Many designers calculate power requirements correctly but fail to account for temperature derating. A power supply rated for 1000W at 25°C may only deliver 600W at 60°C ambient. Always check the derating curve and design for worst-case operating temperature including enclosure effects. For industrial applications, I recommend sizing power supplies at 50% of rated capacity - this provides margin for temperature, improves reliability, and extends lifetime significantly.
faeInsights.logic: Selection process: First, calculate load power with 30% margin. Second, determine input voltage range for your deployment region. Third, select appropriate safety certification level. Fourth, check derating requirements for operating temperature. Fifth, verify mechanical fit and mounting options. Sixth, consider special features like remote sense or parallel operation.
keyTakeaways: Size power supplies with 30-50% margin for reliability
Check temperature derating curves for actual operating conditions
Select safety certification appropriate for application
Verify mechanical fit early in design process
commonPitfalls: Ignoring temperature derating in sizing calculations
Insufficient safety certification for target markets
bestPractices: Check availability and lead times early
troubleshootingTips: If power supply shuts down, check for overtemperature
Measure actual input voltage under load conditions
Verify cooling airflow is not obstructed

customerCases.challenge: Original power supply failed frequently in hot factory environments due to inadequate temperature derating.
customerCases.solution: Resized power supply with proper temperature derating analysis. Implemented 50% power margin and improved enclosure ventilation.
customerCases.result: Power supply reliability improved from 20,000 hours to over 500,000 hours MTBF. Warranty claims eliminated.

faqs.question: What is the difference between open frame and enclosed power supplies?
faqs.answer: Open frame power supplies are PCB assemblies without an enclosure, offering compact size and lower cost. They require installation in an equipment enclosure for safety. Enclosed power supplies have a metal or plastic case providing protection and can be used as standalone units. Open frame is ideal for OEM equipment with existing enclosures. Enclosed is better for standalone or retrofit applications.
faqs.decisionGuide: Select open frame for OEM integration, enclosed for standalone applications.

faqs.question: How much power margin should I design into my system?
faqs.answer: Industry best practice is 20-30% power margin for commercial applications and 30-50% for industrial or high-reliability applications. This margin accommodates: Component tolerance variations; Load transients and peak demands; Temperature derating requirements; Future expansion needs. Higher margin improves reliability and extends power supply lifetime.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use 20-30% margin for commercial, 30-50% for industrial applications.

articles.title: Power Supply Thermal Design Guide
articles.summary: Comprehensive guide for thermal design of power supply systems including heatsink selection, airflow calculations, and reliability optimization.
articles.content: Proper thermal design is critical for power supply reliability and lifetime. This guide covers thermal calculations, cooling methods, and design optimization. Heat Generation: Power supplies generate heat due to inefficiency. Calculate power loss as Ploss = Pout × (1-η)/η where η is efficiency. For example, a 1000W supply at 90% efficiency generates 111W of heat. Temperature Rise: Temperature rise depends on thermal resistance: ΔT = Ploss × Rthermal. Thermal resistance includes junction-to-case, case-to-heatsink, and heatsink-to-ambient components. Cooling Methods: Natural convection - simplest but limited to about 50-100W for enclosed supplies; Forced air cooling - increases cooling capacity 2-5x depending on airflow; Heatsink mounting - baseplate cooled supplies transfer heat to external heatsink; Liquid cooling - for very high power density applications.

relatedArticles.title: How to Select TDK-Lambda AC-DC Power Supplies

faeInsights.insight: Thermal design is often the most overlooked aspect of power system design, yet it's the number one cause of field failures. The biggest mistake is using ambient temperature instead of actual operating temperature. Inside an enclosure, temperature can be 20-30°C higher than room ambient. Another common error is underestimating thermal resistance - the interface between power supply and heatsink often adds significant resistance if not properly managed with thermal grease or pads. My rule of thumb: if you can't hold your hand on the power supply comfortably, it's too hot for long-term reliability.
faeInsights.logic: Thermal design process: First, calculate actual power dissipation from efficiency. Second, determine maximum allowable temperature rise from rated temperature. Third, calculate required thermal resistance. Fourth, select cooling method and components. Fifth, verify design through measurement. Sixth, implement temperature monitoring if critical.
keyTakeaways: Use actual operating temperature, not ambient
Thermal interface resistance is often significant
Temperature directly affects reliability and lifetime
commonPitfalls: Using ambient instead of operating temperature
troubleshootingTips: Use thermal camera to identify hot spots

customerCases.challenge: Power supplies overheating in outdoor enclosures exposed to direct sunlight, causing frequent shutdowns.
customerCases.solution: Redesigned thermal management with larger heatsinks, forced air cooling, and reflective enclosure coating. Implemented temperature monitoring.
customerCases.result: Operating temperature reduced by 25°C. Power supply reliability improved from 2 years to 10+ years MTBF.

faqs.question: How do I calculate required heatsink size?
faqs.answer: Heatsink thermal resistance requirement: Rthermal = (Tmax - Tambient) / Ploss. For example, if maximum case temperature is 85°C, ambient is 45°C, and power loss is 50W: Rthermal = (85-45)/50 = 0.8°C/W. Select heatsink with thermal resistance less than calculated value, including margin for thermal interface.
faqs.decisionGuide: Calculate required thermal resistance and select heatsink with adequate margin.

faqs.question: What is the effect of temperature on power supply lifetime?
faqs.answer: Power supply lifetime decreases exponentially with temperature following the Arrhenius equation. Typically, lifetime halves for every 10°C increase. A capacitor rated for 10,000 hours at 105°C will last approximately 20,000 hours at 95°C, 40,000 hours at 85°C, etc. This is why thermal design is critical for reliability.
faqs.decisionGuide: Design for lowest practical operating temperature to maximize lifetime.

articles.title: Medical Power Supply Design Considerations
articles.summary: Guide for designing medical power systems compliant with IEC 60601-1 including isolation, leakage current, and risk management requirements.
articles.content: Medical power supply design requires special consideration for patient safety under IEC 60601-1. This guide covers key requirements and design practices. Isolation Requirements: 2xMOPP (Means of Patient Protection) is required for patient-connected equipment. This provides two independent protection measures between mains and patient. Isolation must withstand 4000V AC test voltage. Leakage Current: Strict limits apply - 100μA normal condition, 500μA single fault for Type B/BF; 10μA normal, 50μA single fault for Type CF. Calculate total system leakage including all components. Risk Management: ISO 14971 risk analysis is required, documenting hazards and mitigations. Power system hazards include electric shock, fire, and mechanical risks.

relatedArticles.title: How to Select TDK-Lambda AC-DC Power Supplies

faeInsights.insight: Medical power design is fundamentally about patient safety, not just functionality. The most common mistake is trying to use industrial power supplies in medical applications to save cost - this almost always fails regulatory review and ends up costing more in redesign and delays. Key insight: start with certified medical power supplies from the beginning. The 2xMOPP isolation and low leakage current requirements drive transformer design, filtering, and layout decisions that cannot be easily retrofitted. Another critical point: risk management is now mandatory under IEC 60601-1 3rd edition. You must document how your power system design addresses safety hazards.
faeInsights.logic: Medical power design process: First, determine patient contact type (B, BF, CF) to establish isolation requirements. Second, calculate leakage current budget for entire system. Third, select certified medical power supplies with appropriate isolation. Fourth, design grounding and shielding for EMC and safety. Fifth, prepare risk management documentation. Sixth, plan regulatory testing including single fault conditions.
keyTakeaways: Use certified medical power supplies - don't design your own
commonPitfalls: Using industrial supplies in medical applications
bestPractices: Calculate leakage current from day one
troubleshootingTips: If leakage is high, check filter capacitors and grounding

customerCases.challenge: Previous power design failed IEC 60601-1 leakage current requirements and took 12 months to redesign.
customerCases.solution: Implemented CUS series medical power supplies with complete documentation. Redesigned grounding and filtering for low leakage.
customerCases.result: Passed IEC 60601-1 testing on first attempt. FDA clearance achieved in 4 months. Product launched successfully.

faqs.question: What is 2xMOPP isolation?
faqs.answer: 2xMOPP (Means of Patient Protection) provides two independent protection measures between mains and patient. First protection is reinforced insulation in transformer and optocouplers. Second protection is protective earth connection. This ensures safety even if one protection fails. Required for all patient-connected medical equipment.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use 2xMOPP certified power supplies for all patient-connected equipment.

faqs.question: How do I calculate medical leakage current?
faqs.answer: Medical leakage current includes earth leakage, enclosure leakage, and patient leakage. Sum contributions from all components including power supplies, filters, and cables. Limits: Type B/BF - 100μA normal, 500μA single fault; Type CF - 10μA normal, 50μA single fault. Include 50% safety margin in design.
faqs.decisionGuide: Calculate total leakage early and select low-leakage components.

articles.title: DC-DC Converter Application Guide
articles.summary: Application guide for DC-DC converters including selection, filtering, layout, and distributed power architecture design.
articles.content: DC-DC converters are essential building blocks in distributed power systems. This guide covers selection and application best practices. Converter Types: Isolated converters provide galvanic isolation between input and output, breaking ground loops and providing safety isolation. Non-isolated converters (buck, boost) are more efficient and compact but share common ground. Selection Criteria: Input voltage range must accommodate source variations; Output power rating with appropriate margin; Isolation voltage for safety requirements; Efficiency for thermal management; Package size for space constraints. Input Filtering: DC-DC converters draw pulsating current requiring input capacitors to handle ripple current and prevent input voltage droop. Calculate required capacitance based on ripple current and allowable voltage ripple.

faeInsights.insight: The most common DC-DC application issue is insufficient input filtering. Designers often underestimate the pulsating input current and its effects. A DC-DC converter switching at 200kHz can draw current in narrow pulses, creating high RMS ripple current that heats capacitors and generates EMI. My rule: always use low-ESR ceramic or polymer capacitors at the input, placed close to the converter pins. Another frequent issue is layout - long traces between converter and load create voltage drops and noise pickup. Keep output traces short and wide, and use remote sense if voltage accuracy is critical.
faeInsights.logic: DC-DC design process: First, determine isolation requirements for your application. Second, calculate input and output voltage ranges. Third, size converter with 20-30% power margin. Fourth, design input filtering for ripple current. Fifth, optimize PCB layout for low impedance. Sixth, add output filtering if ripple requirements are stringent.
keyTakeaways: Input filtering is critical for reliable operation
Use low-ESR capacitors close to converter pins
Keep output traces short and wide
Use remote sense for voltage accuracy
bestPractices: Use ceramic or polymer input capacitors
Place capacitors close to converter pins
Minimize trace lengths on input and output
troubleshootingTips: Check input voltage ripple with oscilloscope

customerCases.challenge: DC-DC converters failing due to input voltage transients and insufficient filtering in noisy industrial environment.
customerCases.solution: Redesigned input filtering with ceramic and electrolytic capacitors. Added TVS protection. Improved PCB layout.
customerCases.result: Converter reliability improved 10x. System passed industrial EMC testing. Zero field failures in 2 years.

faqs.question: When should I use isolated vs non-isolated DC-DC converters?
faqs.answer: Use isolated converters when: Breaking ground loops between subsystems; Safety isolation is required; Connecting to external equipment with different grounds; Creating floating outputs. Use non-isolated converters when: Input and output share common ground; Maximum efficiency is required; Cost and size are critical; No safety isolation needed.
faqs.decisionGuide: Select isolated for safety and ground loop breaking, non-isolated for cost-sensitive common-ground applications.

faqs.question: How much input capacitance do I need for DC-DC converters?
faqs.answer: Input capacitance depends on ripple current and allowable voltage ripple. General guideline: 10-100μF per ampere of input current for buck converters; Use low-ESR ceramic (1-10μF) in parallel with electrolytic for best performance; Place capacitors within 5mm of converter input pins. Calculate: Cin = Iripple / (8 × fsw × ΔVripple) for buck converters.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use 10-100μF per ampere with low-ESR ceramic close to converter pins.
